paragraphs.body: Shadows Under the Skyscrapers - The Dark Side of the City The name “Iron City” was ingrained in my mind before I left for college in Pittsburgh. I imagined the second-largest city in Pennsylvania brimming with towering skyscrapers like a bustling metropolis, like Beijing. The city, I thought, would be vibrant and modern, with sparkling glass buildings and clean, organized streets. I imagined people living affluent lives, traveling between sleek office buildings, shopping in upscale stores, and enjoying lavish cafés. Like other major financial centers, I expected Pittsburgh to exude affluence and sophistication, However, when I finally set foot in Pittsburgh, the fantasy was mercilessly shattered by reality. While there were indeed skyscrapers in the city center, they were not as tall or as opulent as I had imagined. Compared to the modern skyscrapers that have risen in Beijing, the buildings here looked old, bearing the marks of age and erosion. The mottled paint and slightly crumbling bricks on the façades of many buildings seemed to tell the story of Pittsburgh’s former glory as an industrial center. They no longer symbolized modernity and prosperity but rather bore witness to the city's transformation from the industrial age to the present day. The streets were not as busy as I had expected; instead, they were cold and lonely. The sky was filled with cigarette smoke, and the occasional passerby walked briskly, head down, blending into the gray tone of the city.

annotations.comment: Please specify what you mean by "they."

paragraphs.body: What shocked me even more was that the decay of the city was obvious. Abandoned stores and dilapidated apartments stand next to luxurious office buildings, and the dusty merchandise seems to have been untouched for a long time. The yellowed and curled advertisements on the windows and doors seemed to tell the story of a once prosperous place that is now in a state of depression. Walking along the streets, I saw some ragged vagrants, huddled in the corners.

Underneath the towering skyscrapers, I realized there was another side of city life I hadn’t known: the poor, struggling beneath the city’s glamorous exterior. Beneath the shell of Pittsburgh’s skyscrapers lies a deeply heartbreaking picture. Their eyes revealing their confusion and helplessness towards the future. People looked like they were struggling hard to make a living, with a hint of fatigue and bewilderment in their expressions. All of this was completely different from the Pittsburgh of opportunity and vitality that I had in mind. Pulling me back from my fantasy, under the shadow of the skyscrapers, there is another kind of city life that is not known to me: the poor people who are struggling hard under the glamorous exterior. Underneath the shell of the city of skyscrapers lies such a heartbreaking picture. Such a sight reminds me of Beijing, a city also surrounded by skyscrapers. Although Pittsburgh and Beijing are far apart in terms of historical background and geographic location, they are surprisingly similar in some ways. For example, 国贸大厦 (Beijing's China World Trade Center) is surrounded by modern skyscrapers that sparkle in the sunlight, highlighting the city's prosperity and vitality. People here live in a fast-paced environment, with vehicles and people constantly on the move, almost resembling the saying 车水马龙. The streets are always filled with activity, whether it’s early in the morning or late at night.

Compared to Pittsburgh, however, Beijing has a very different atmosphere. Day and night, the streets are filled with people, and it’s hard not to think of the expression 熙熙攘攘. During the day, the city is busy with traffic and people hurrying about their lives, while at night, it transforms into a different world. The colorful neon lights that illuminate the city give it a surreal, almost dreamlike quality, perfectly described by the phrase 如梦如幻 (as if in a dream). It’s a city that never seems to sleep, constantly alive with energy and vibrancy.

Even behind this bustle, you can still see the old neighborhoods that hold countless memories. There are 胡同 (hutongs) with narrow alleys, mottled walls, and residents who still retain their traditional way of life. In these hutongs, you can see old people sitting in front of their houses chatting about their families, and children playing carefree in the alleys. There is no urban hustle and bustle here, but more of a peaceful state of life. These working families living on the edge of the city live ordinary and authentic lives in the rapidly developing city of Beijing. This contrast between the old and the new, the prosperous and the simple, outlines a three-dimensional and rich Beijing, as if it somehow has a subtle resonance with Pittsburgh.

annotations.comment: Since it's a personal narrative, please use the first-person perspective.

paragraphs.body: In terms of architectural style, the two cities display very different qualities. Beijing's new buildings emphasize modernity and futurism, with uniquely shaped, avant-garde skyscrapers that seem to speak silently of the city's development. Pittsburgh's buildings, on the other hand, are more like a microcosm of the industrial age, heavy and sturdy, with a hint of vicissitudes, as if silently recording the city's past. The linguistic differences are even more obvious. Although English and Mandarin are the languages of a modern metropolis, I felt a linguistic and cultural divide from Beijing to Pittsburgh, which deepened my understanding of the different cultures of the two places.

Through this cross-cultural comparison, I have begun to re-examine my views of these two cities. In the past, I always thought that Western cities such as Pittsburgh were bound to be full of wealth and opportunities, while Chinese cities were fast-growing but lagging in some respects. However, this experience has completely overturned these notions. The silence of Pittsburgh and the bustle of Beijing made me realize that the development of cities and the distribution of wealth are not as simple as they seem but are influenced by complex historical and social factors.

Pittsburgh's quietness may be a settling down after the glory of its industrial age, while Beijing's vigor stems from its rapid development in recent decades. Each city has its own unique trajectory, which is not only related to its economy, but also reflects the influence of culture, policies, and other aspects. The comparison made me realize that stereotypes often mask the diversity and complexity of cities. The West is not always prosperous, and the East is not always backward. Understanding a city requires going beyond the surface and delving into its inner social and cultural context. 正所谓“看人挑担不吃力，事非经过不知难” (As the saying goes, "It’s easy to watch someone carry a load, but you won’t know the difficulty until you’ve experienced it yourself."). Through this comparison, I realized that the surface of cities often hides deeper social realities. Although Pittsburgh and Beijing are very different in terms of history and culture, they both hide the gap between the rich and the poor and social inequality under the shadow of skyscrapers. Behind the skyscrapers, there are many people struggling to make ends meet, and these are things we tend to overlook when we first observe a city. Through this cross-cultural experience, I began to rethink my view of the city and society. Language and culture are more than just superficial differences; they are reflections of people's real lives. Different ways of expression and living habits are responses to the inner state of life in their respective cities.

annotations.comment: This is a well-written paper. Please avoid vague pronouns such as "they" and use the first-person perspective and simple past tense consistently throughout the paper. Overall, good job!

paragraphs.body: This experience has given me a deeper understanding of cultural diversity, and it has also made me realize that every city has its own unique story and complex reality, whether it is a bustling metropolis or a seemingly downtrodden neighborhood. It’s easy to think a city is prosperous just by looking at its skyscrapers, but the real story lies in the people who live there and their daily struggles. It is through the surface impressions of cities that we can truly understand them and feel the vitality and challenges hidden in the depths. By looking closer, we realize that cultural understanding becomes more three-dimensional and gives us a more comprehensive view of how people live in different environments.
